mappings.rationaleAI: History of global exploration from ancient expeditions to volcanic and space exploration covers surface exploration (A), undersea exploration through maritime voyages (B), and space exploration (C).

Geology covering Earth's deep interior, surface features, resource discovery, and exploitation directly addresses surface and underground exploration (A), scientific determination of Earth's interior properties (A.1.a), resource exploration for ores, fuels, and water (A.1.b), and exploration methods (A.2).

Seismology, gravity, geomagnetism, and satellite-based measurement address scientific exploration of Earth's interior (A.1.a), indirect geophysical methods (A.2.a), and navigational methods for precise location (B.2).
Plate tectonic theory explaining lithospheric movement, subduction, and geological processes directly addresses scientific determination of the properties of Earth's interior (A.1.a).

Ocean science covering seafloor topography, currents, tides, and marine resources addresses undersea exploration (B), platforms for exploratory work (B.1), and oceanographic sampling and measurement (B.3).
Marine ecosystem diversity and ocean circulation connect to undersea exploration (B) and developments in oceanographic measurement (B.3).

Antarctic exploration covering glaciology, paleoclimate ice core research, and connections to outer space research addresses surface exploration (A) and contributions to physical sciences from polar exploration (C.4).
Arctic physical geography including ice masses, permafrost, and environmental transformation addresses surface exploration techniques and equipment (A).

Planetary science covering the Solar System, exoplanets, and the Hubble Space Telescope addresses space exploration (C), unmanned spacecraft probing planets (C.2.b.iii), and contributions to physical sciences (C.4).
Natural satellites studied through space missions including New Horizons address unmanned spacecraft probing planets and their satellites (C.2.b.iii) and contributions to physical sciences (C.4).
Formation and evolution of planetary systems including exoplanetary discovery through modern telescopes addresses space exploration (C) and contributions to physical sciences (C.4).
Solar physics covering observational methods and solar missions addresses orbiting satellites for scientific purposes (C.2.b.ii) and contributions to physical sciences (C.4).
Physical cosmology studying the universe's origin and evolution, including Hubble Space Telescope contributions, addresses space exploration's contributions to physical sciences (C.4).
Physics of stars, galaxies, and cosmos using observational methods across the electromagnetic spectrum addresses contributions of space exploration to advances in physical sciences (C.4).
Search for life elsewhere in the solar system through comparative planetology addresses unmanned spacecraft probing planets (C.2.b.iii) and contributions to physical sciences (C.4).

Atmospheric science covering composition, greenhouse effect, and remote sensing addresses scientific exploration of Earth's properties (A.1.a) and sounding rockets exploring the upper atmosphere (C.2.b.i).

Interdisciplinary study of Earth as an integrated system covering atmosphere, biosphere, and oceans addresses scientific exploration of Earth's properties (A.1.a) and connects to contributions of space observation to Earth science (C.4).
Mineralogy covering mineral classification and geochemistry connects to resource exploration for ores and building materials (A.1.b).
Maritime navigation covering instruments and charting connects to navigational methods for establishing precise location in undersea exploration (B.2).
Space-based telescopes operating across the electromagnetic spectrum address orbiting satellites for scientific purposes (C.2.b.ii) and contributions to advances in physical sciences (C.4).
Volcanic processes as planetary-scale heat release mechanisms revealing tectonic forces address scientific exploration of Earth's interior properties (A.1.a).
Geology of rocks covering deep Earth composition and extraterrestrial meteorites addresses scientific exploration of Earth's interior (A.1.a) and resource exploration for building materials (A.1.b).
History of astronomy from prehistoric observations through post-WWII developments connects to the history of space flight theory (C.1) and contributions to physical sciences (C.4).

mappings.vsiAuthor: David George Bowers, Emyr Martyn Roberts
mappings.rationaleAI: Tidal dynamics in oceans including observation methods and effects on Jupiter and Saturn moons connect to undersea exploration (B) and developments in oceanographic measurement (B.3).
Robotic deployment for planetary space exploration and autonomous machines connect to unmanned underwater vehicles (B.1.d) and unmanned spacecraft probing planets (C.2.b.iii).
